posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Honestly, this is about the best thing I’ve seen a wine critic do in recent times. Perhaps partly it is due to William providing such depth of attention and context to a region that other critics do not, and most do not seem to understand, at least not very well. Partly it is due to William’s lexicon and ability to describe impressions and thoughts in such a very visually appealing way. And of course, with that smug, refined British accent and his Oxfordian mannerisms, you just know that this cat is smarter and more cultured than you. And yes, I don’t mind his little jab on the Bouland, “no overt sucrosity of fruit . . . .” “I have a few for my cellar . . . cases . . . .” LOL, love it.

Seriously, this is worth every second of the 8-minute watch. Would love to see more content delivered in this fashion. It would be worth the price tag, even for those of us that actually do not subscribe to anything. So cool if more critics and winemakers would do this now during our shelter-in-place time for CV19 (yes, Fu, I am doing some sheltering).

These are three of my annual Beaujolais buys, in addition to Roilette, Thivin, Lapierre and occasionally, Vissoux. Very cool to see these gems get in-depth coverage. World class wine at very nice pricing.
Yeah it was a nice piece. Thanks for sending the link!
Awesome! Thx for posting.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: Fun video, but is it OK to drink Beaujolais if you did not attend Oxford or Cambridge?

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Fun video, but is it OK to drink Beaujolais if you did not attend Oxford or Cambridge?

Actually, I believe that graduates must receive special dispensation from their institution to drink the stuff. You know the rules, claret and port.

posts.name: A G Aguirre
posts.cooked: Great video

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked:
These are three of my annual Beaujolais buys,…

But, but…do you really enjoy Metras with age? I find they are best out of the gate.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: 100%, Marcus. I was just staring at some 2015 Metras a few minutes ago, jonesing for a quality Beaujolais. I have no basis to say this other than gut instinct, but I think they are made to be enjoyed promptly and storage carries some long-term risk. And they are amazingly expressive and aromatic on release, so for me, why wait.

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked: i had a 12 metras fleurie not long ago that was sublime. better than several bottles of 11 ultime. i am sure they age quite well, but not without a certain risk.
I recently drank through a few 1997 Metras Fleurie. Every bottle was fantastic. Exotic and delicious. Still very well put together.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Ok I’m eating some words on Metras, inspired by William to pop one today:

WineBerserkers – 25 May 17

TN: 2014 Yvon Métras Fleurie L'Ultime - Huh, $75 bucks for a stinkin'...

Yea, you might think we’re nuts. Beaujolais is Coca-Cola wine, right? Hah. Not so fast, sonny, though on reflection, $75 is still a bit nutty. Metras is a singular wine. I think stylistically it sorta transcends Beaujolais. It is Beaujolais,...

2014 Metras l’Ultime.

Granted, this is supposedly the flagship cuvee, and it ain’t cheap!

Robert - I believe that is Oxonian mannerisms… (but, hey, the Brits are proving as stupid as we are/vice versa).

You are calling William a cow, a steer? I mean, I know I’m a yak, but William is above that fray. Maybe a Texas Longhorn? That’s a lot of bull. Damn this Ultime is wonderful, just finishing glass three.

posts.name: C Chen

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Thanks very much for all the kind words, everyone! That’s very encouraging.

I’m stuck in Beaune but I have a lot of wine to taste through for various articles this Spring, as well as a nicely stocked cellar, so I will make a few more videos. Did one on old Beaujolais (as a counterpoint to the 2018s) last night which I’ll post soon.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked: I’m not a Beaujolais drinker but enjoyed the video anyway - thanks and well done, William!

posts.name: Rich Salsano
posts.cooked: Great video. I watched yesterday. Glad to hear good things about 2018 and it sounds as though it confirms my understanding of it being more of a drink early vintage.

posts.name: Josh Grossman
posts.cooked:
You all inspired me to pop a ‘18 tonight:

Not too ripe and nice tannins on the end. Built to age and on the Pinot side of the curve.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Oh that’s cool, the La Chappelle cuvee!!

That is one of the real highlights of the range in 2018!

William -

What’s the story behind all these Thivin cuvees now? I’ve been buying Thivin for as long as I can remember, and these cuvees like La Chappelle, Zachariah, Griottes, Bertrand, et al, just seemed to pop up out of nowhere. Perhaps they were made before and my go-to retailers did not carry them. I have the 2017 Zachariah last year and it was flat out stunning. Also, is there a hierarchy among them, or just different vineyards?

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Ok dummy me, google is my friend. An excellent website in English, describing quite nicely the range of wines and the details of all recent vintages:

Château Thivin

Our wines - Château Thivin

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Oh that’s cool, the La Chappelle cuvee!!

That is one of the real highlights of the range in 2018!

William -

What’s the story behind all these Thivin cuvees now? I’ve been buying Thivin for as long as I can remember, and these cuvees like La Chappelle, Zachariah, Griottes, Bertrand, et al, just seemed to pop up out of nowhere. Perhaps they were made before and my go-to retailers did not carry them. I have the 2017 Zachariah last year and it was flat out stunning. Also, is there a hierarchy among them, or just different vineyards?

As you’ve seen, the website explains the particularities of the various lieu-dit cuvées. None of them are directly exported to the USA, however, so anything you are finding is not coming via Kermit.

posts.name: Laurent Gibet
posts.cooked:
William Kelley on Instagram

Honestly, this is about the best thing I’ve seen a wine critic do in recent times. Perhaps partly it is due to William providing such depth of attention and context to a region that other critics do not, and most do not seem to understand, at least not very well. Partly it is due to William’s lexicon and ability to describe impressions and thoughts in such a very visually appealing way. And of course, with that smug, refined British accent and his Oxfordian mannerisms, you just know that this cat is smarter and more cultured than you. And yes, I don’t mind his little jab on the Bouland, “no overt sucrosity of fruit . . . .” “I have a few for my cellar . . . cases . . . .” LOL, love it.

Seriously, this is worth every second of the 8-minute watch. Would love to see more content delivered in this fashion. It would be worth the price tag, even for those of us that actually do not subscribe to anything. So cool if more critics and winemakers would do this now during our shelter-in-place time for CV19 (yes, Fu, I am doing some sheltering).

These are three of my annual Beaujolais buys, in addition to Roilette, Thivin, Lapierre and occasionally, Vissoux. Very cool to see these gems get in-depth coverage. World class wine at very nice pricing.

Métras Fleurie Printemps 2017 : 14/20 (july 2020)
Clean but quite weak.

Foillard once again to “nature” for me (like Chamonard) :
Foillard Morgon Côte du Py 2018 : 15,5/20 puis quasi ED – 20/10/2020
Plus joyeux/fruité que La Sagesse 2018 de Gramenon, heureusement. Défaut le lendemain (éthanal, pomme blette).

I recently discovered an interesting producer :
Arnaud Combier Chiroubles 2018 : 16/20 (very good ripe but fresh and thin wine)

Arnaud Combier Fleurie 2018 : 15,5/20 – 23/5/2020 (october 2020)
14,5°. Bien fait, très mûr mais sans lourdeur obsédante (opulent mais fin). Fait pinot noir (fleurs, cerise confite).

Arnaud Combier Beaujolais Villages premières gouttes 2018 : 15/20 (november 2020)
Belle invitation aromatique : fruit particulièrement mûr signant le millésime (cerise confite), épices, violette, réglisse.
Bouche nette, équilibrée (belle acidité pour le millésime – 12,5° d’alcool annoncés), lisse, gourmande. Longueur correcte.
